title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
Your school has asked students to write a report about recycling habits in the school. Your report should describe the current situation, explain why recycling is important, and suggest ways to improve recycling in the school.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Recycling Habits at Westfield Secondary School

Introduction
The purpose of this report is to describe current recycling practices at our school, assess their effectiveness, and recommend improvements.

Current Situation
At present, the school has recycling bins for paper and plastic in the corridors. However, many students do not use them correctly. Observations show that general waste and recyclable materials are frequently mixed together, reducing the effectiveness of the scheme.

Importance of Recycling
Recycling helps reduce the amount of waste sent to landfill and lowers greenhouse gas emissions. For a school with over 800 students, small changes in behaviour could make a significant environmental difference.

Recommendations
Firstly, the school should organise a short assembly to educate students about what can and cannot be recycled. Secondly, clearer labels and colour-coded bins would help avoid confusion. Finally, a student-led 'Green Team' could monitor the bins and raise awareness throughout the year.

Conclusion
With these simple measures, our school could significantly improve its environmental impact and serve as a model for other institutions.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read an article in a local newspaper suggesting that people do not care about the environment. Write a letter to the newspaper editor expressing your views on environmental responsibility, giving examples, and saying what individuals can do to help.

Write your letter.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I am writing in response to the article published last week claiming that people no longer care about the environment. I strongly disagree with this view, and I believe it overlooks the many positive changes happening in our communities.

From my own experience, I can see clear signs of growing environmental awareness. My neighbours have recently installed solar panels on their roof. My school has started a composting project, and many of my friends have given up single-use plastic bottles.

Of course, there is still much more to be done. Individuals can make a real difference by making small, consistent choices: using reusable bags, reducing meat consumption, and choosing public transport over private cars.

However, responsibility should not rest solely on individuals. Governments and businesses must also take bold action, including investing in renewable energy and imposing stricter regulations on pollution.

I hope this letter encourages your readers to feel empowered rather than hopeless. Every action, no matter how small, contributes to a healthier planet.

Yours faithfully,
Maria Gonzalez

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'We are looking for articles about ways young people can help protect the environment in their daily lives. What can teenagers do to make a real difference?'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Small Steps, Big Impact: How Teenagers Can Help the Planet

Many young people feel that environmental problems are too large for individuals to solve. However, the truth is that small, everyday choices can add up to make a huge difference. So what can teenagers actually do?

The most straightforward step is to reduce waste. This means refusing unnecessary plastic bags, carrying a reusable water bottle, and thinking carefully before buying new clothes. Fast fashion is one of the world's biggest polluters, and simply buying second-hand items can significantly reduce your personal impact.

Another powerful action is to raise awareness. Sharing reliable information about climate change on social media, organising school events, or joining local environmental groups are all excellent ways to inspire others.

Finally, teenagers can put pressure on decision-makers. Writing to local politicians, signing petitions, or participating in peaceful demonstrations sends a clear message that young people demand action.

Environmental responsibility is not only for scientists or politicians. It belongs to all of us — and it starts today.
